did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study compared the diagnostic accuracy of human readers against 139 machine-learning algorithms created by 77 labs for classifying pigmented skin lesions (dermatology task) using dermatoscopic images. The AI methods were part of the International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2018 challenge.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The article is titled 'Comparison of the accuracy of human readers versus machine-learning algorithms for pigmented skin lesion classification: an open, web-based, international, diagnostic study.' It presents original experimental results from a diagnostic study comparing human readers against 139 machine-learning algorithms, including specific methods, findings with statistical metrics (e.g., p<0.0001), and interpretations, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Comparison of the accuracy of human readers versus machine-learning algorithms for pigmented skin lesion classification: an open, web-based, international, diagnostic study
brief_summary: This study compared the diagnostic accuracy of state-of-the-art machine-learning algorithms against human readers for classifying pigmented skin lesions using dermatoscopic images. The research utilized data from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2018 challenge, involving 139 algorithms from 77 labs and 511 human readers across various specialties. The evaluation was conducted via an open, web-based, international diagnostic study where participants diagnosed images from a test set of 1511 images. The results showed that machine-learning algorithms significantly outperformed all human readers, with the top three algorithms achieving a mean of 25.43 correct diagnoses compared to 18.78 for human experts with over 10 years of experience. However, the performance gap narrowed when images were from sources not included in the training set, indicating decreased algorithm performance on out-of-distribution data. The authors conclude that while AI classifiers currently surpass human experts, their limitations with novel data sources must be addressed for broader clinical adoption.

all_task_types.target: Pigmented skin lesion diagnosis (one of seven predefined disease categories: intraepithelial carcinoma, basal cell carcinoma, benign keratinocytic lesions, dermatofibroma, melanoma, melanocytic nevus, or vascular lesions)
all_task_types.details: The study evaluates 139 machine-learning algorithms for classifying pigmented skin lesions from dermatoscopic images into discrete diagnostic categories. The primary outcome measured was the number of correct specific diagnoses per batch, indicating a multi-class classification task.

datasets_used.name: International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2018 Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset used for the ISIC 2018 challenge, consisting of dermatoscopic images of pigmented skin lesions. It was split into a training set provided to machine-learning labs and a test set used for the diagnostic study.
classes_used: Intraepithelial carcinoma (including actinic keratoses and Bowen’s disease)
Benign keratinocytic lesions (including solar lentigo, seborrheic keratosis, and lichen planus-like keratosis)

use.training: The training set of 10,015 images was provided in advance to 77 machine-learning labs to create 139 algorithms.
use.testing: A test set of 1,511 images was used for the open, web-based, international diagnostic study where 511 human readers diagnosed images in 30-image batches.
use.other: The test set was further analyzed to compare performance on images from sources included in the training set versus those from sources not included in the training set (out-of-distribution data).

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: number of correct specific diagnoses per batch

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To estimate the uncertainty around the mean number of correct diagnoses for machine-learning algorithms and human readers, and to quantify the difference in performance between human experts and algorithms.
To determine the statistical significance of the difference in diagnostic accuracy between machine-learning algorithms and human readers (all human readers and human experts).
To quantify the absolute difference in the number of correct specific diagnoses per batch between human readers/experts and machine-learning algorithms.
To measure the variability or dispersion of the number of correct diagnoses among human readers and machine-learning algorithms.

hypothesis_testing.test: Statistical comparison of mean correct diagnoses (p-value reported)
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in the mean number of correct specific diagnoses per batch between machine-learning algorithms and human readers (or between human experts and the top three algorithms).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a difference in the mean number of correct specific diagnoses per batch between machine-learning algorithms and human readers (specifically, that algorithms outperform humans).
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The study reported p<0.0001 for the comparison between all human readers and all machine-learning algorithms (mean difference 2.01, 95% CI 1.97 to 2.04) and for the comparison between human experts and the top three algorithms (mean difference 6.65, 95% CI 6.06–7.25), indicating that the performance of machine-learning algorithms was significantly higher than that of human readers.

ISIC 2018 Challenge Test Set (1511 image.metric: Mean correct specific diagnoses per batch (30 images)
ISIC 2018 Challenge Test Set (1511 image.notes: Machine-learning algorithms significantly outperformed all human readers. The performance gap narrowed for out-of-distribution images: human underperformance was 11.4% (95% CI 9.9–12.9) for images from sources not included in the training set, compared to 3.6% (95% CI 0.8–6.3) for images from sources included in the training set (p<0.0001).

architecture_details.note: The paper evaluates 139 machine-learning algorithms created by 77 different labs for the International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2018 challenge. The specific architectures, model families, or technical details of these individual algorithms are not described in this text; the study focuses on comparing their aggregate diagnostic accuracy against human readers rather than detailing their internal structures.

training_details.training_data: 10,015 dermatoscopic images of pigmented skin lesions provided in advance to the 77 machine-learning labs.
training_details.note: Specific training hyperparameters, optimizers, loss functions, or schedules for the 139 algorithms are not reported in this paper.

output_classes: Intraepithelial carcinoma (including actinic keratoses and Bowen’s disease)
Benign keratinocytic lesions (including solar lentigo, seborrheic keratosis, and lichen planus-like keratosis)
classification_or_output_head.note: The algorithms performed multi-class classification into one of seven predefined disease categories. Specific classifier head details are not reported.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: 139 machine-learning algorithms created by 77 machine-learning labs for the International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2018 challenge
conclusions: State-of-the-art machine-learning classifiers outperformed human experts in the diagnosis of pigmented skin lesions.
Machine-learning algorithms should have a more important role in clinical practice.
A limitation of these algorithms is their decreased performance for out-of-distribution images (images from sources not included in the training set).
The decreased performance on out-of-distribution data should be addressed in future research.
important_remarks: The study highlights a significant limitation regarding out-of-distribution data: the performance gap between human experts and top machine-learning algorithms narrowed significantly for images from sources not included in the training set (human underperformance of 11.4% vs 3.6% for in-distribution images, p<0.0001).

The machine-learning algorithms were developed by 77 different labs for the International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2018 challenge, meaning the specific architectures and internal details of the 139 algorithms are not standardized or detailed in this paper.

The diagnostic study was conducted via an open, web-based platform where human readers diagnosed images in 30-image batches, which may introduce variability in reading conditions compared to controlled clinical settings.

The human reader cohort included 511 participants from 63 countries with diverse backgrounds (55.4% board-certified dermatologists, 23.1% residents, 16.2% general practitioners), which may limit the generalizability of the 'human expert' benchmark to highly specialized dermatologists alone.

The study relies on a single test set of 1511 images; while the training set contained 10,015 images, the external validation on out-of-distribution sources suggests potential overfitting to the specific distribution of the training data.
